tasks.prompt: Our Qdrant node has 128 GB RAM, but memory looks full most of the time and occasionally crosses into OOM risk during optimizations. The collection has 70M 1024-d vectors, several payload indexes, and quantization enabled. How do we tell what must stay in RAM and reduce the footprint safely?

rubric.text: Distinguishes resident memory (RSSAnon: ID tracker, quantized vectors with always_ram, payload indexes) from OS page cache (original vectors, releasable); notes page cache filling RAM is normal while resident >80% is the real problem, checked via /metrics

Attributes the OOM-during-optimization risk to insufficient headroom: optimized segments are fully loaded into RAM during optimization, so leave headroom (and a smaller max_segment_size reduces the headroom needed)

Since quantization is already enabled, recommends further footprint levers: original vectors / HNSW / payload indexes on disk, float16 or int8 datatypes (2x/4x reduction), or MRL to keep only small vectors in RAM
Notes payload indexes and the HNSW graph also consume resident memory and must be counted in the estimate, not just raw vectors
Mentions async_scorer (io_uring, Linux 5.11+) and moving sparse/text payload to disk to keep on-disk performance acceptable
Suggests confirming quantized vectors are the copy kept in RAM (always_ram) while originals stay on disk / page cache
Concludes a memory leak because page cache fills available RAM
Only recommends adding RAM / a bigger node and ignores the explicit footprint-reduction ask

tasks.prompt:
Running a 3-node Qdrant cluster (v1.17.0) with 15GB RAM per node, 60+ collections, 3 shards + replication factor 3 per collection, dynamically created collections. Started hitting recurring startup/recovery crashes with errors like: 'Cannot allocate memory (os error 12) failed to set up alternative stack guard page'. The nodes still had ~14GB available RAM, while the Qdrant process showed very high VSZ (~71GB) but low RSS (~4GB), so this appears related to mmap/virtual memory pressure rather than actual RAM exhaustion. Reducing max_workers didn't help the node start. Log at crash time:

```
2026-05-18T11:40:16.613636Z  INFO qdrant: Telemetry reporting disabled
2026-05-18T11:40:16.613968Z  INFO qdrant::tonic: TLS disabled for internal gRPC API
2026-05-18T11:40:16.617711Z  WARN qdrant::actix::web_ui: Static content folder for Web UI './static' does not exist
2026-05-18T11:40:16.617840Z  INFO qdrant::actix: TLS disabled for REST API
2026-05-18T11:40:16.617880Z  INFO qdrant::actix: Qdrant HTTP listening on 6333
2026-05-18T11:40:16.617884Z  INFO actix_server::builder: starting 2 workers
2026-05-18T11:40:16.617889Z  INFO actix_server::server: Actix runtime found; starting in Actix runtime
2026-05-18T11:40:16.617895Z  INFO actix_server::server: starting service: "actix-web-service-10.164.204.99:6333", workers: 2, listening on: 10.164.204.99:6333
2026-05-18T11:40:16.621945Z  INFO qdrant::tonic: Qdrant gRPC listening on 6334
2026-05-18T11:40:16.621954Z  INFO qdrant::tonic: TLS disabled for gRPC API
2026-05-18T11:40:19.961747Z ERROR qdrant::startup: Panic backtrace: [unknown frames]
2026-05-18T11:40:19.961761Z ERROR qdrant::startup: Panic occurred in file library/std/src/sys/pal/unix/stack_overflow.rs at line 240: failed to set up alternative stack guard page: Cannot allocate memory (os error 12)
fatal runtime error: failed to initiate panic, error 5, aborting
```

Is there a way to make the cluster stable in this situation?

rubric.text: Suggests checking the kernel mmap limit via `cat /proc/sys/vm/max_map_count` as a potential cause
Mentions low_memory_mode (available in v1.18) as a way to help stabilize this setup
Notes that creating a high number of collections is not recommended
Notes the crash occurs after/during collection loading at startup
